frozen_components: Decoder copy used in auxiliary loss (stop-gradient)
architecture.summary: Gated Sparse Autoencoder (Gated SAE) for unsupervised discovery of interpretable features in language model activations. The system separates feature detection from magnitude estimation via a gated encoder: a gating path (with Heaviside step) determines which features are active, while a magnitude path (ReLU) estimates their coefficients. Both paths share encoder projection directions but differ in bias and scaling parameters (weight tying reduces parameter overhead from 50% to ~25%). The decoder linearly reconstructs activations from active features. Training applies L1 sparsity penalty only to gating pre-activations, with an auxiliary decoder task to guide magnitude estimation without the sparsity penalty.
trained_components: Gated encoder (shared projections, dual biases, scaling parameter γ)

biblio.title: Improving Dictionary Learning with Gated Sparse Autoencoders

claims.dataset: GELU-1L, Pythia-2.8B, Gemma-7B (multiple layers and sites)
claims.statement: Gated SAEs require half as many firing features to achieve comparable reconstruction fidelity compared to baseline SAEs.
Gated SAEs achieve a Pareto improvement over baseline SAEs holding training compute fixed: they yield sparser decompositions at any desired level of reconstruction fidelity.
Gated SAEs solve shrinkage (systematic underestimation of feature activations) compared to baseline SAEs, with relative reconstruction bias approaching 1.0.

claims.dataset: Pythia-2.8B (150 features, 5 raters), Gemma-7B (192 features, 7 raters)
claims.statement: Gated SAE features are comparably interpretable to baseline SAE features according to expert human raters in a double-blind study.
Training a Gated SAE requires 50% more compute than training a matched-width baseline SAE due to dual decoder passes in the auxiliary loss.
The performance improvement of Gated SAEs over baseline SAEs is not solely attributable to addressing shrinkage; a significant portion comes from learning better encoder and decoder directions.
Freezing the decoder in the auxiliary task for training the magnitude path is beneficial; unfreezing it reduces performance.
We do not see evidence that Gated SAEs overfit when reconstructing activations despite increased expressivity via the Jump ReLU discontinuity.
